    19→- [x] Bibliography entry present: `[Al63] Altman, E., _On a problem of P. Erdős_. Amer. Math. Monthly 70 (1963), 148-157.`

    22→

    28→

    30→- [x] `distinctDistances` has a LaTeX docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
    31→- [x] `distinctDistances` is marked `noncomputable` (uses `Finset.image` with `dist`, which requires classical decidable equality).
    32→- [x] `InConvexPosition` is not marked `noncomputable` — correct, it returns `Prop` and needs no computability.

    35→- [x] `distinctDistances` (returns `Finset ℝ`) uses `lowerCamelCase` — correct.
    36→- N/A: No local notation used (none needed for this problem).

    38→

    40→
    41→- [x] Uses the `theorem` keyword.
    42→- [x] Main theorem named `erdos_93` (snake_case, matches namespace number).
    43→- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research solved, AMS 52]`.
    44→- [x] Has a docstring (`/-- ... -/`) describing the result.
    45→- [x] Docstring references the source paper: `[Al63]`.
    46→
    47→### `answer()` usage
    48→
    49→The problem is stated as a direct assertion: "If n points in convex position, then they determine at least ⌊n/2⌋ distinct distances." This is **Case 3** — a direct assertion, not a yes/no question or a "find the value" problem.
    50→
    51→- [x] The theorem type is `P` directly (no `answer()` wrapper) — correct for Case 3.
    52→- [x] Quantifiers are theorem parameters (standard Lean/Mathlib style for Case 3 with no `answer()`).
    53→
    54→## 7. Category Attribute
    55→
    56→- [x] Exactly one `@[category ...]` attribute present.
    57→- [x] Value is `research solved` — valid category; the problem was proved by Altman (1963).
    58→- N/A: Not `research open`, so no sorry-free-proof check needed.
    59→
    60→## 8. AMS Attribute
    61→
    62→- [x] Exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute present.
    63→- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed: `52`.
    64→- [x] Numbers in ascending order (single number).
    65→- [x] No duplicate numbers.
    66→- [x] `52` (Convex and discrete geometry) is a valid AMS MSC2020 code — appropriate for a problem about convex position and distances.
    67→
    68→## 9. Proof Bodies
    69→
    70→- [x] Uses `by` / `sorry` in the multi-line format (`:= by\n  sorry`) — acceptable per checklist.
    71→- [x] No `sorry` in `def` or `abbrev` declarations.
    72→- [x] No informal proof sketches included.
    73→- [x] No `native_decide`.
    74→
    75→## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems
    76→
    77→- N/A: No variants present. The problem is stated as a single theorem.
    78→
    79→## 11. Code Quality
    80→
    81→- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
    82→- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `ℝ`, `∀`, `∈`, `∉`, `≤`.
    83→- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
    84→- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
    85→- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all well under 100 characters).
    86→- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.
    87→

    89→

    92→
    93→## Summary
    94→
    95→**All checkable items pass.** The file is clean and well-structured. The formalization correctly defines convex position and distinct distances as separate helper definitions with appropriate docstrings, then states the main theorem as a direct assertion (Case 3) with `category research solved`. The AMS code `52` (Convex and discrete geometry) is appropriate. No issues found.

file.content:
# Review: deepmind/93.lean

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 93`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link: `*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/93](https://www.erdosproblems.com/93)`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem (convex position implies ⌊n/2⌋ distinct distances).
- [x] Bibliography entry present: `[Al63] Altman, E., _On a problem of P. Erdős_. Amer. Math. Monthly 70 (1963), 148-157.`

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos93 ... end Erdos93`.
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase`: `Erdos93`.
- [x] `open scoped Classical` appears **before** the namespace block.

## 5. Custom Definitions

- [x] `InConvexPosition` has a LaTeX docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
- [x] `distinctDistances` has a LaTeX docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
- [x] `distinctDistances` is marked `noncomputable` (uses `Finset.image` with `dist`, which requires classical decidable equality).
- [x] `InConvexPosition` is not marked `noncomputable` — correct, it returns `Prop` and needs no computability.
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions.
- [x] `InConvexPosition` (a `Prop`) uses `UpperCamelCase` — correct.
- [x] `distinctDistances` (returns `Finset ℝ`) uses `lowerCamelCase` — correct.
- N/A: No local notation used (none needed for this problem).
- N/A: No `@[category test]`/`@[category API]` sanity checks added (optional).

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Uses the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem named `erdos_93` (snake_case, matches namespace number).
- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research solved, AMS 52]`.
- [x] Has a docstring (`/-- ... -/`) describing the result.
- [x] Docstring references the source paper: `[Al63]`.

### `answer()` usage

The problem is stated as a direct assertion: "If n points in convex position, then they determine at least ⌊n/2⌋ distinct distances." This is **Case 3** — a direct assertion, not a yes/no question or a "find the value" problem.

- [x] The theorem type is `P` directly (no `answer()` wrapper) — correct for Case 3.
- [x] Quantifiers are theorem parameters (standard Lean/Mathlib style for Case 3 with no `answer()`).

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[category ...]` attribute present.
- [x] Value is `research solved` — valid category; the problem was proved by Altman (1963).
- N/A: Not `research open`, so no sorry-free-proof check needed.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute present.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed: `52`.
- [x] Numbers in ascending order (single number).
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] `52` (Convex and discrete geometry) is a valid AMS MSC2020 code — appropriate for a problem about convex position and distances.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Uses `by` / `sorry` in the multi-line format (`:= by\n  sorry`) — acceptable per checklist.
- [x] No `sorry` in `def` or `abbrev` declarations.
- [x] No informal proof sketches included.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- N/A: No variants present. The problem is stated as a single theorem.

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `ℝ`, `∀`, `∈`, `∉`, `≤`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all well under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` not run — requires build environment.
- [ ] Custom linters not verified — requires build environment.

## Summary

**All checkable items pass.** The file is clean and well-structured. The formalization correctly defines convex position and distinct distances as separate helper definitions with appropriate docstrings, then states the main theorem as a direct assertion (Case 3) with `category research solved`. The AMS code `52` (Convex and discrete geometry) is appropriate. No issues found.

content.text: The review passed — all checkable items pass with no issues found. No changes needed.
